sections.content:

It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The character moved from cols 20-21 to cols 50-51 after 5 RIGHT actions. Each RIGHT moves about 5 columns. The character is now near the right edge of the blue region.
Looking at the image, I can see the character is now near the narrow passage area. The narrow passage is at rows 30-36, cols 48-52. The character is at rows 37-40, cols 50-51.
But wait - the character can only move left/right, not up/down. So how does it reach the upper blue area?
Let me look at the image more carefully. The character is at the right side of the lower blue area. The narrow passage above connects to the upper blue area. But the character can't move up.
Maybe the goal is just to move the character to specific horizontal positions to "collect" or "align with" the green blocks? Let me check if moving to certain positions triggers something.
Looking at the green block positions:
- Upper 3 blocks: cols 13-17, 19-23, 25-29 (rows 1-5)
- Lower 4 blocks: cols 31-35, 37-41, 43-47, 49-53 (rows 13-17)
The character is currently at cols 50-51, which aligns with the rightmost green block (cols 49-53).
Maybe the goal is to move the character to align with each green block horizontally? Let me try moving to align with the other green blocks and see if anything happens.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the game mechanics. The character moves left/right within the blue region. The green blocks are above the blue region. Maybe the goal is to position the character below each green block and then do something (like press ACTION7 or MOUSE) to "collect" it?
But I already tested ACTION7 and MOUSE, and they only affected the HUD bar. Unless they have a different effect when the character is aligned with a green block.
Let me try ACTION7 now that the character is aligned with the rightmost green block (cols 49-53).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Character is now at cols 50-51, aligned with rightmost green block (cols 49-53)
# Let me try ACTION7 while aligned with a green block to see if it does something different

result = action(['ACTION7'])
print(f"After ACTION7 at cols 50-51: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")

# Check if green blocks changed
seg = current_frame.segmentation
green_blocks = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Green blocks: {len(green_blocks)}")
for n in green_blocks:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    print(f"  N: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check character position
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        b = n['boundary']
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Character: cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
        break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
